authoritative_sources.title: Google Cloud Architecture Framework
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official architecture framework for Google Cloud workload design quality and tradeoff language across system design, operational excellence, security, reliability, cost, and performance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: scope intake, option framing, tradeoff analysis, architecture decision output

authoritative_sources.title: Google Cloud Architecture Framework: Operational excellence
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official operations guidance for observability, automation, incident readiness, operational readiness, and continuous improvement in Google Cloud architectures.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: operability design, monitoring plan, logging plan, runbook output, change validation

authoritative_sources.title: Google Cloud Architecture Framework: Reliability
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official reliability guidance for failure domains, service limits, monitoring, backup, disaster recovery, resiliency testing, and recovery objectives.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: resilience review, DR planning, failure-mode analysis, backup and recovery evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Landing zone design in Google Cloud
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official Architecture Center guidance for landing zones, resource hierarchy, networking, identity, logging, security, and operating model foundations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: landing zone intake, organization and project topology, guardrail review, shared-services architecture

authoritative_sources.title: Virtual Private Cloud documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official source for VPC networks, subnets, routes, firewalls, Cloud NAT, Private Service Connect, and service connectivity used in Google Cloud network architecture.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: network architecture evidence intake, segmentation design, routing and endpoint recommendation

authoritative_sources.title: Shared VPC documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official documentation for host projects, service projects, centralized network administration, and Shared VPC attachment behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: host and service project topology, shared-services design, network administration boundary handling

authoritative_sources.title: Network Connectivity Center overview
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official documentation for hub-and-spoke network connectivity, hybrid attachments, and centralized connectivity behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: hub-and-spoke design, hybrid connectivity, blast-radius and route-domain review

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Monitoring documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official observability documentation for metrics, alerting policies, SLOs, dashboards, and uptime checks used as runbook evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: observability intake, SLO and alert design, runbook evidence, incident-triage guidance

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Logging documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official logging documentation for log routing, sinks, audit logs, retention, exclusions, and operational evidence boundaries.

authoritative_sources.title: Disaster recovery planning guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official Architecture Center guidance for disaster recovery scenarios, RTO/RPO planning, recovery strategies, failover, and restore testing.

authoritative_sources.title: Migration to Google Cloud
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official migration workflow guidance for assessment, workload discovery, migration planning, waves, cutover, and modernization sequencing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: migration intake, dependency analysis, sequencing, cutover validation, rollback planning

authoritative_sources.title: Terraform Google Provider Documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp official Google provider reference for Terraform-managed Google Cloud resources, provider behavior, and supported resource semantics.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Terraform architecture review, provider-version caveats, resource behavior validation

authoritative_sources.title: Google Kubernetes Engine documentation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud official GKE documentation for managed Kubernetes context, used to decide when GKE placement remains a Google Cloud architecture decision and when cluster operations should route to the Kubernetes specialist.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: container platform placement review, GKE architecture context, adjacent-lane routing

authoritative_sources.title: Kubernetes Architecture
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Kubernetes project documentation is the recognized upstream reference for Kubernetes control-plane and node architecture, useful for GKE architecture boundary handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: GKE boundary handling, container platform handoff, upstream terminology check

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 500-292: NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST official cross-industry cloud reference architecture defines cloud actors, activities, roles, and responsibility vocabulary for cloud architecture boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cross-industry cloud terminology, role-boundary handling, provider-consumer-auditor separation

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST official control catalog gives recognized evidence, monitoring, contingency, configuration, audit, access, and risk terminology for cross-industry cloud evidence handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control and evidence prerequisite review, caveat handling, conflict escalation

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Google Cloud Platform Foundations Benchmark
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Google Cloud Platform Foundations Benchmark is a recognized secure-configuration benchmark for Google Cloud organization, project, IAM, logging, monitoring, networking, and storage baseline controls, useful as evidence context for guardrail discussions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: guardrail evidence review, security-boundary handoff, configuration baseline caveats

authoritative_sources.title: Current google-cloud-architecture-specialist package baseline
authoritative_sources.source_class: Current package task baseline from google-cloud-architecture-specialist
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The committed legacy package is authoritative only for preserving current supported tasks, input and output shapes, validation threshold, refusal rules, and no-regression context.

workflow_stages.name: scope, authority, and Google Cloud context intake
workflow_stages.description: Confirm Google Cloud scope, request intent, desired output, workload owner, business outcome, resource hierarchy boundary, region, tenancy model, data classification, source precedence, and adjacent-lane indicators before producing architecture guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: tenant evidence and system-of-record assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect Resource Manager, organization policy, VPC, Shared VPC, connectivity, workload, IAM touchpoint, backup, monitoring, logging, cost, IaC, change, incident, ADR, diagram, policy, and exception records from tenant-approved systems.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: source selection and precedence review
workflow_stages.description: Choose current Google Cloud, HashiCorp, Kubernetes, NIST, CIS, and tenant sources; record freshness, source scope, conflicts, and whether tenant or contractual policy overrides public baseline guidance.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: architecture option and tradeoff analysis
workflow_stages.description: Evaluate organization, folder, project, network, workload placement, resilience, observability, migration, cost, isolation, quota, data residency, and IaC options against stated constraints and failure modes.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: implementation, migration, and operability sequencing
workflow_stages.description: Prepare dependency order, implementation notes, validation checkpoints, rollback moments, observability and backup requirements, DR tests, and owner handoffs without executing changes.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: artifact delivery, refusal, or orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Deliver an evidence-linked architecture recommendation, migration plan, operability guide, IaC review, or explicit refusal and escalation note with confidence state and unresolved assumptions.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: organization, folder, project, and landing-zone topology map
resource hierarchy, organization policy, and guardrail review notes
VPC, Shared VPC, Network Connectivity Center, endpoint, routing, and isolation topology recommendation
operability, monitoring, logging, backup, incident, and DR runbook guidance
cost, reliability, isolation, residency, and quota tradeoff matrix
Terraform, Config Controller, or Deployment Manager IaC review notes
boundary refusal, escalation, or adjacent-lane handoff note
systems_of_record: Google Cloud Resource Manager organization, folders, projects, tags, labels, and organization policies
Google Cloud landing-zone, project vending, project factory, service account, IAM touchpoint, shared-services, and guardrail records

VPC, Shared VPC, Network Connectivity Center, Cloud Interconnect, Cloud VPN, Cloud Router, Cloud DNS, Private Service Connect, Cloud NAT, load balancing, subnet, route, firewall, and endpoint inventories

Cloud Asset Inventory, Cloud Audit Logs, Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, Error Reporting, Backup and DR Service, Cost Management, budgets, quota records, service usage, and architecture review records
Terraform state and plans, Config Controller resources, Deployment Manager legacy state, IaC repositories, CI/CD logs, and change-management tickets
Architecture diagrams, ADRs, CMDB records, workload dependency inventories, incident tickets, runbooks, backup and restore evidence, exception registers, and tenant policies

allowed: Recommend Google Cloud organization, landing-zone, network, workload, resilience, migration, operations, cost-performance, and IaC architecture options.
Prepare implementation guidance, validation checkpoints, rollback moments, evidence requests, and owner handoffs.
Identify architecture risks, tradeoffs, assumptions, and domain failure modes without approving execution or accepting risk.
escalate: Required Google Cloud tenant facts, evidence, source freshness, source precedence, data classification, RTO/RPO, SLO, region, residency, or owner authority is missing.
Google Cloud documentation, tenant policy, contractual requirements, customer commitments, regulatory obligations, or reviewer instructions conflict.
The request needs architecture review board, change advisory board, security, identity, network, FinOps, legal, compliance, procurement, or executive approval.
refuse: Application-only engineering implementation, security investigation, enterprise identity policy ownership, general financial forecasting, legal advice, procurement approval, compliance attestation, production execution, or risk acceptance.
Requests to execute infrastructure changes, bypass change control, deploy IaC, perform failover, alter projects, override guardrails, or approve spend.
Requests to treat generic cloud advice as Google Cloud architecture without Google Cloud-specific records, artifacts, systems, and decision boundaries.

domain_failure_modes: Generic cloud guidance ignores Google Cloud organization, folder, project, region, zone, service quota, data residency, or service-limit constraints.
Landing-zone recommendation collapses organization, folder, project, service-account, organization-policy, logging, and guardrail authority boundaries.
Network topology advice omits Shared VPC host and service project behavior, routes, endpoints, DNS, inspection, hybrid connectivity, or blast-radius consequences.
Reliability design recommends RTO/RPO, DR, or backup patterns without tenant objectives, restore evidence, or failure-domain analysis.
Migration sequencing ignores dependencies, data movement, cutover validation, rollback moments, or change windows.
IaC review overlooks Terraform state, Config Controller reconciliation, Deployment Manager legacy behavior, drift detection, policy guardrails, or blast radius.
Cost guidance becomes financial approval instead of technical cost-performance evidence.
Security, identity, network operations, Kubernetes operations, legal, compliance, or production execution work is absorbed instead of routed.
Public guidance and tenant policy conflict without precedence resolution.
Cross-industry evidence appears complete but lacks named Google Cloud systems of record or owner authority.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Google Cloud Resource Manager, landing-zone and project vending records, organization policies, VPC and Shared VPC inventories, Network Connectivity Center, Cloud Asset Inventory, Cloud Audit Logs, Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, Backup and DR records, quota and cost records, Terraform state, Config Controller resources, Deployment Manager legacy state, ADRs, diagrams, runbooks, change records, policies, exception registers, and owner approval records govern the lane.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Google Cloud architecture fails materially when organization hierarchy, folder or project ownership, Shared VPC host-service boundaries, region and zone availability, quotas, routes, endpoints, log routing, backup behavior, IaC state, shared responsibility, and owner authority details are absent; generic cloud advice can create wrong blast radius, cost, resilience, or compliance assumptions.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Application engineering, cloud security, incident response, identity architecture, network operations, Kubernetes platform operations, FinOps approval, procurement, legal, compliance attestation, production execution, risk acceptance, and architecture approval boards constrain the lane.

unresolved_ambiguities.notes: No blocking ambiguity remains for the spec pack. At runtime, conflicting Google Cloud guidance, tenant policy, contractual obligations, regulatory requirements, or reviewer instructions require precedence resolution before deterministic recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Google Cloud service availability, quotas, pricing, and preview features
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: No blocking ambiguity remains for the spec pack. Runtime materialization must refresh Google Cloud service availability, region support, quotas, pricing, and preview-feature status when those facts affect the answer.
This package remains specification-based. Retaining a trusted generated implementation requires separate frequent-use evidence and approval.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated spec-pack release because tenant-specific uncertainty is represented as runtime prerequisites, refusal triggers, or orchestrator return conditions.

The source set covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling using Google Cloud official documentation, HashiCorp official provider documentation, Kubernetes upstream documentation, NIST public standards, CIS benchmark guidance, and the current package baseline only for regression preservation.
